title: Straight-Beam Examination: Evaluating Results and Reporting
narrationScript: Finding a signal is the easy half of straight beam work. This lesson is about the other half, deciding what the signal is, whether the procedure says to record it, and writing a report another technician could stand behind years from now. Start with the vocabulary, because the words carry legal weight. Anything that produces a response on the screen is an indication. An indication that comes from an actual feature in the material is a discontinuity. A discontinuity only becomes a defect when it fails the acceptance criteria, and that judgment belongs to the evaluation step, not to the first glance. Your screen will also show signals that come from the shape of the part rather than from a flaw, edge echoes, echoes from fillets or drilled holes, and mode converted signals that wander in after the back wall on long parts. Those are nonrelevant. You prove a signal is geometric by plotting where it comes from and checking it against a drawing of the part, never by just assuming. Now evaluation. The procedure gives you objective recording criteria, and for straight beam laminar work they usually come in two flavors. The first is amplitude based. You compare the echo height to the reference you set during calibration, and the procedure tells you the recording level, for example record any indication that reaches half of the reference amplitude. The second watches the back wall. A lamination that is tilted or full of scattered porosity may throw very little energy back at the probe, but it robs the back wall, so procedures commonly require you to record any area where the back reflection drops below half of what it was on good material, even if no new echo appears ahead of it. Six decibels means a factor of two in amplitude, so dropping from eighty percent of full screen height to forty percent is a six decibel loss, exactly half. When an indication crosses the recording threshold, you stop scanning and you size it. The standard field method is the six decibel drop. Peak the echo, note the amplitude, then move the probe until the echo falls to half its peak height. At that point the beam centerline is roughly over the edge of the reflector. Mark the spot, work around the whole indication the same way, and you have traced its boundary. Measure the position of that boundary from the reference datum the procedure names, a plate corner, a weld seam, a stamped grid line. Depth comes straight off the screen from the calibrated range. Record the maximum amplitude, where it occurred, the extent in both directions, the depth, and how much back reflection remained. Then comes the decision. Depending on how your written practice assigns duties, a Level One records and reports to a Level Two, and the Level Two or Three compares the recorded indications to the acceptance criteria and writes the disposition. Acceptance for plate commonly turns on the size of the area you mapped, whether the lamination fits inside a circle of a stated diameter, or whether it sits close to an edge where a weld will later go. You do not need to memorize any one product standard here. You need the habit, map honestly, record completely, and let the criteria do the judging. Finally the report. A test report is not a formality, it is the only thing that survives the job. The test of a good report is simple, could another qualified technician repeat your examination from the paper alone and get the same answer. That means the report identifies the part, the material and thickness, the procedure and its revision, and the acceptance criteria used. It identifies the instrument by make, model, and serial number, the transducer by frequency, size, type, and serial number, the calibration standard by its identification, and the couplant. It states the surface condition and any preparation, the scan plan and coverage, the reference gain and the scanning gain, and the times of every calibration confirmation. For results, it shows each recorded indication with location from the datum, amplitude relative to reference, extent, depth, and remaining back reflection, usually on a sketch or map of the part. And it ends with the disposition against the criteria, your name, your certification level, your signature, and the date. Corrections get a single line through the error, initialed, never correction fluid. If it is not written down, it did not happen. Write reports you would be proud to defend in five years, because one day you may have to.
imagePrompt: A technician kneels on a large steel plate marking a chalk outline around a mapped lamination area while filling in an examination report on a clipboard, flaw detector screen glowing beside the probe.

sections.heading: Indication, Discontinuity, Defect
sections.body:
Evaluation starts with disciplined vocabulary, because these words carry contractual weight:

- **Indication** — any response on the screen that demands attention. Nothing more is implied.
- **Discontinuity** — an actual interruption in the material's structure, confirmed to be the source of an indication.
- **Defect** — a discontinuity that has been **evaluated against the acceptance criteria and failed**. Only then is the part rejectable.

Straight-beam screens also carry **nonrelevant indications** that come from the part itself: echoes from edges and corners when the beam clips them, reflections from machined fillets, grooves, or drilled holes, and mode-converted signals that arrive after the back-wall echo in long forgings and shafts. The dead zone under the initial pulse can likewise hide or distort near-surface responses.

The professional rule: a signal is proven geometric by **plotting its origin** — using the calibrated range and the probe position to locate the reflector, then checking that location against the part drawing — never by assuming. Misjudging a lamination as an edge echo is how rejectable plate gets welded into a vessel.

sections.heading: Recording Criteria: Amplitude and Back-Reflection Loss
sections.body:
The procedure converts judgment into numbers. For straight-beam laminar work, recording criteria usually take two complementary forms:

- **Amplitude-based.** Compare echo height with the calibration reference. A typical instruction: record any indication equal to or exceeding **50% of reference amplitude** (procedures vary — some record at 20%, evaluate at 50%, reject at 100%).
- **Back-reflection loss.** A tilted or scattering lamination may return little energy to the probe while still robbing the back wall. Procedures therefore commonly require recording any area where the back-wall echo falls below a stated fraction — often **50% of its level on adjacent sound material** — even with no new echo ahead of it. Remember the dB arithmetic: a drop from 80% FSH to 40% FSH is **6 dB, a factor of two** in amplitude.

Before recording either condition, eliminate the innocent causes — couplant starvation, scale, paint, pitting, probe wear — by re-coupling and checking adjacent material. A recordable condition is one that **persists under good coupling**.

sections.caption: Evaluation screen for a straight-beam scan: the indication at 62% FSH exceeds the 40% recording level (half of the 80% reference), while grass and geometry signals sit below it.

sections.title: Let the criteria do the judging
sections.body: Product standards for plate and forgings define acceptance in measurable terms — commonly whether the mapped laminar area fits within a circle of stated diameter, with tighter limits near edges that will carry welds. Your duty is the honest map and the complete record; **acceptance or rejection is read from the governing specification**, not from how dramatic the screen looks. Per documents such as SNT-TC-1A, a Level I records and reports results; evaluation against criteria belongs to Level II or III.

sections.heading: Sizing and Mapping with the 6 dB Drop
sections.body:
Once a signal crosses the recording threshold, size it. The standard field method for reflectors **larger than the beam** is the **6 dB drop**:

1. Maneuver the probe to **peak** the echo and note the amplitude.
2. Move the probe outward until the echo falls to **half its peak height** (−6 dB). The beam centerline now sits approximately over the reflector's edge.
3. Mark the part at the probe centerline; repeat around the indication until the **boundary is traced**.

Measure the traced boundary from the **reference datum** the procedure names — a stamped plate corner, a centerline, a grid system — so the location survives after your chalk washes off. Read **depth** directly from the calibrated screen range at the indication's position. Record the maximum amplitude and its location, length and width of the mapped area, depth, and the percentage of back reflection remaining over the indication. Intermittent responses along a line are recorded with their start and stop points; the procedure states when closely spaced indications are treated as continuous.

sections.caption: Mapping a lamination for the report: the 6 dB drop boundary is referenced to the plate datum with X-Y coordinates, extent, depth, and remaining back reflection recorded.

sections.heading: The Test Report: One Document, Whole Examination
sections.body:
The report is the only part of the examination that survives the job, and it may be read by a code inspector, a customer auditor, or a failure investigator years later. The standard it must meet is **repeatability**: another qualified technician should be able to reproduce your examination from the paper alone.

That drives the required entries. Identify the **work**: part number and description, material specification, thickness, heat or serial number, surface condition, and any surface preparation. Identify the **method**: procedure number and revision, acceptance criteria reference, technique (contact straight beam), scan plan and coverage achieved, couplant, and reference plus scanning gain in dB. Identify the **system**: instrument make, model, and serial number; transducer frequency, element size, type, and serial number; calibration standard identification; and the time of every calibration confirmation with its result. Then the **results**: each recorded indication with datum-referenced location, amplitude versus reference, extent, depth, and back-reflection loss — almost always on a sketch or plate map. Close with the **disposition** against the criteria and the examiner's printed name, certification level, signature, and date.

Correct errors with a single line, initialed and dated — never obliterate an entry. Reports are retained per the quality system; treat every one as a future exhibit.

sections.caption: Minimum entries on a straight-beam examination report, grouped by what they identify.
rows: Part/heat ID, material spec, thickness, surface condition and preparation
Procedure number and revision, acceptance criteria reference
Instrument make/model/serial, transducer frequency/size/type/serial, calibration standard ID, couplant
Sweep range, reference gain (dB), scanning gain, calibration confirmation times and results
Scan plan (grid or 100%), overlap, surfaces scanned, any inaccessible areas
Each indication: datum location, amplitude vs. reference, extent, depth, back-reflection loss; sketch/map
Disposition, examiner name, certification level, signature, date

sections.heading: Field Habits That Make Reports Defensible
sections.body:
A few working habits separate a report that survives an audit from one that does not:

- **Record as you go.** Reconstructing locations and amplitudes at the end of a shift produces fiction, not data.
- **Map on the part first, paper second.** Chalk or paint-stick the 6 dB boundary, photograph it where permitted, then transfer measured coordinates to the sketch.
- **Write the back-reflection percentage**, not just "BW loss" — 45% remaining and 5% remaining are different findings.
- **Note everything abnormal**, even below recording level, when the procedure asks for it: clusters of small indications can matter to the reviewing Level III.
- **Document the inaccessible.** If clamps, attachments, or surface condition prevented coverage, the unscanned area is reported, not ignored.
- **Never let the disposition lead the data.** Production pressure to write "accept" is real; the measurements go down as measured, and the criteria decide.

If it is not written down, it did not happen — and what is written down must have happened exactly as written.

figures.title: Evaluation screen: recording level and reference level
Plate map and report sketch for a lamination

interactive.title: Which Signal Gets Recorded?
interactive.intro: Your procedure set the back-wall reference at 80% FSH and requires recording any indication at or above 50% of reference — the 40% FSH dashed line. Click the signal on this evaluation screen that the procedure requires you to record.

regions.label: Initial pulse
regions.feedback: That is the initial pulse at the entry surface — present on every contact test. It is not an indication from the material; near-surface flaws hide in its dead zone, which is a reason duals exist.

regions.label: Grass at about 12% FSH
regions.feedback: Low-level grain scatter sits far below the 40% recording level. You note general noise level if the procedure asks, but it is not recordable.

regions.label: Indication at 62% FSH
regions.feedback: Correct. 62% FSH exceeds the 40% recording level (50% of the 80% reference). Stop scanning, peak it, size the boundary with the 6 dB drop, and record location, amplitude, extent, depth, and remaining back reflection.

regions.label: Back-wall echo at 85% FSH
regions.feedback: A healthy back wall at about the reference height is the expected signal. You would record the back wall only where its amplitude drops below the procedure's loss threshold.

checkQuestions.question: An echo peaks at 80% FSH. Using the 6 dB drop method to find the reflector's edge, you move the probe until the echo reads:
options: 74% FSH
60% FSH
40% FSH
20% FSH
checkQuestions.explanation: 6 dB is a factor of two in amplitude, so the edge is marked where the echo falls to half its peak: 80% ÷ 2 = 40% FSH, with the beam centerline approximately over the reflector edge.

checkQuestions.question: On a long shaft, a clean signal appears after the back-wall echo and cannot be plotted to any position inside the part. It is most likely:
options: A defect requiring immediate rejection
A nonrelevant mode-converted or geometric signal, to be verified by plotting against the part geometry
Proof the instrument is broken
A lamination deeper than the part is thick
checkQuestions.explanation: Signals arriving after the back wall in long parts are classic mode-converted or geometry-related responses. They are proven nonrelevant by plotting their origin against the drawing — not assumed, and not rejected as flaws.

checkQuestions.question: The reference back wall was set at 80% FSH on sound material. Scanning, the back wall falls to 32% FSH over one area with good coupling and no new echo. The procedure records back-reflection loss exceeding 50%. You:
options: Do nothing — there is no flaw echo to record
Record the area: 32% is 40% of the 80% reference, more than 50% loss
Increase the gain until the back wall returns to 80% and continue
Record it only if a second technician sees it too
checkQuestions.explanation: 32 ÷ 80 = 40% remaining, a loss of 60% — beyond the 50% threshold. Tilted or scattering laminations often show only as back-reflection loss, which is exactly why the criterion exists.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of report entries does the repeatability standard make mandatory?
options: Weather, shift length, and the examiner's opinions of the part
Instrument make/model/serial, transducer details, calibration standard ID, reference gain, and procedure revision
Only the indications that exceeded acceptance criteria
The price of the examination and the customer contact
checkQuestions.explanation: Another qualified technician must be able to duplicate the examination from the report alone, which requires the complete system identification, settings, and governing documents — not just the findings.

checkQuestions.question: Under a typical written practice based on SNT-TC-1A, who evaluates recorded straight-beam indications against the acceptance criteria?
options: The Level I who found them
A Level II or Level III
The production foreman
Whoever signs the shipping papers
checkQuestions.explanation: A Level I performs examinations and records results to a specific procedure; interpretation and evaluation against acceptance criteria are Level II/III functions under the written practice.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, UT Technique Course §3.3 (evaluation of results), §3.4 (test reports)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 4 (examination records, general practice)
Plate product standards (general practice): back-reflection-loss recording and circle-based laminar acceptance
SNT-TC-1A (Level I/II/III duties with respect to recording and evaluation, general framing)
